summarization.a: Ball-and-stick model of graphene oxide (GO) supercell with an ordered distribution of epoxy groups on one side, showing slight basal-plane buckling.
summarization.b: Ball-and-stick model of GO with ordered hydroxyl groups on one side, illustrating structural distortion due to hydrogen bonding.
summarization.c: Ball-and-stick model of GO with mixed epoxy and hydroxyl groups on one side, showing combined effects on planarity and surface geometry.
summarization.d: Ball-and-stick model of GO with epoxy groups on both sides of the sheet, highlighting increased buckling compared to single-side functionalization.
summarization.e: Ball-and-stick model of GO with hydroxyl groups on both sides, showing enhanced basal-plane distortion due to hydrogen bonding interactions.
summarization.f: Ball-and-stick model of GO with mixed epoxy and hydroxyl groups on both sides, illustrating complex buckling patterns and local structural perturbations.
summarization.g: Ball-and-stick model of epoxy-doped GO used for investigating oxygen doping effects on ALD reactivity, showing planar distortion at doped sites.
summarization.h: Ball-and-stick model of another epoxy-doped GO variant, highlighting local structural modifications and surface buckling relevant for ALD chemistry.

a.question: Do single-sided versus double-sided functionalizations affect the structural planarity of graphene oxide?
a.answer: Yes. Double-sided functionalizations, especially with mixed distributions, tend to increase buckling compared to single-sided, ordered functionalizations.

b.question: How does the distribution of epoxy and hydroxyl groups influence the planarity of GO layers?
b.answer: Ordered distributions of epoxy and hydroxyl groups preserve planarity, while random or mixed distributions increase sp3 carbon content and cause buckling.

d.question: Explain how the choice of GO functionalization impacts its suitability for ALD seeding layers.
d.answer: Functionalization affects both planarity and uniformity of the GO surface, which are critical for atomic layer deposition (ALD) applications. Ordered single- or double-sided functionalizations maintain a mostly planar layer, allowing for uniform seeding and controlled film growth. In contrast, random distributions or heavy buckling can hinder film uniformity and thickness control, reducing the effectiveness of GO as a seeding layer. Epoxy-doped models are additionally useful for studying enhanced reactivity in ALD, although their formation is less likely experimentally.

c.question: List the factors in GO models that determine thermal stability and planarity in DFT simulations.
c.answer: Type of functional group (epoxy or hydroxyl), Side-specific functionalization (one or both sides), Distribution pattern (ordered vs. random), Density of functional groups, Epoxy-doped versus hydroxyl-doped modifications
